title: Function reference
text: PdDataSet

title: Function reference
text: PdDataSet

title: Current features of pyLikeSAS
text:
Basic pyLikeSAS object PdDataSet extends the pandas DataFrame class by adding the ability to set the following attributes (stored in the DataFrame’s _attrs attribute):

labels for columns (variables)

formats for columns (variables)

support for SAS‑like dataset options, such as:

a dataset label

drop/keep variables

rename variables

define sort BY order

run SQL SELECT on a dataset (or on multiple tables)


The concept of column formats in pyLikeSAS is implemented using Python dictionaries or mapping functions.
See the provided lookup_format function, which also supports value ranges as well as missing and unknown values for a given variable in a PdDataSet.
Note:
As the PdDataSet class simply extends the pandas DataFrame, all capabilities of the underlying DataFrame remain available.



pyLikeSAS also includes the data_step function, which mimics the behavior of the SAS data step.
The function is designed to work with PdDataSet objects and allows you to define the data‑step logic as a single Python function.
The return values from this logic function are then used to construct the output PdDataSet.
The following data‑step features are supported:

define retain variables

define BY groups (including FIRST./LAST. flag variables)

define output columns and their order in the resulting dataset

pass additional parameters to the data‑step logic function (such as dictionaries, lists, etc.)




The PyODS class is the pyLikeSAS version of the SAS Output Delivery System (ODS). Its goal is to provide simple generation of outputs in various file formats (destinations), with colorful styles selected or defined by the user. It includes:

support for titles and footnotes

table output to the following formats:

plain text files

Jupyter notebooks

terminal output (formatted using the tabulate package)

HTML files

Markdown

LaTeX


figure (graph) output to the following formats:

PNG, JPEG

SVG

PDF


output of graphs either as links or inline content in the generated files

definition of custom styles and templates

tracing of PyODS operations




There is no way to implement even a small fraction of all SAS procedures.
For now, only the basic presentation‑style functions are integrated with the PyODS class:

proc_print — print a table

proc_figure — render a graph from a Python matplotlib figure

In addition, a few functions provide (very limited) functionality similar to their SAS counterparts:

proc_freq1 — one‑dimensional frequency table(s)

proc_freq2 — two‑dimensional frequency table(s)

proc_contents — list the contents of a table: variables and their basic statistics

… more to come




Several additional tools are included in pyLikeSAS:

PLS_logging — logging class for pyLikeSAS

diagnostics / helper functions, such as:

csv_info — get information about a CSV file

sas_sample_datasets — download data from the SAS sample site

python environment info — report details about the current Python environment


… more to come

PdDataSet (pandas dataset) object is small extension of pandas DataFrame, it appends following attributes to columns (variables):

label - descriptive label of the column to be used when PdDataSet is presented (printed)
format - descriptor that defines how the value in column will be presented (when printed)
PdDataSet also support desc attribute which represents description of dataset (could be set e.g as title when printed).

As child object of pandas DataFrame PdDataSet support all methods known to pandas DataFrame. Above attributes of PdDataSet are kept inside object, they are surfaced only when PdDataSet is printed (or used in the graph). When processing PdDataSet (by SQL or data step or using data set options) its attributes are inherited by the output PdDataSet.



Simplest variant - just pass pandas DataFrame

# initialize PdDataSet by passing pd.DataFrame
ds = PdDataSet(df)

Using pandas DataFrame constructor

# initialize PdDataSet using pd.DataFrame constructor options
newcomers_tuples = [
    ('Ferda', 'M', 14, 100.0, 45.00),
    ('Manicka', 'F', 13, 91.0, 40.00)
]
newcomers_ds = PdDataSet(
    newcomers_tuples,
    columns=[
        'Name', 'Sex', 'Age', 'Weight', 'Height'
    ],
)
Both above examples had instantiated PdDataSet object but did not set any additional attributes available to PdDataSet (so these are basically plain pandas DataFrame). Additional PdDataSet attributes are covered in following sections.



Label parameter allows to define descriptive labels of PdDataSet columns. It is just stored as PdDataSet attribute, i.e. original short column names are preserved, column labels are used only when PdDataSet is presented (e.g. by using proc_print).
df1 = PdDataSet(
    df.head(3),
    label={'Name': "Student's First Name", 'Sex': "Student Gender"}
)
proc_print(df1)

Format of variable, as it is known in SAS, is actually mapping of column’s values to the set of descriptive labels. Formats are applied when only data set is presented (e.g. printed by proc_print). Formats are persistent attributes of PdDataSet, i.e. these are preserved when PdDataSet is processed by SQL (used in the ds_options) or data_step function.
Definition of mapping function in python is straightforward, also direct usage of python formatting (like '{:,.1f}') is supported by inheriting it from pandas.

Example of setting PdDataSet with formats

# read input df
df = pd.read_csv("./data/class.csv").head(3)

# provide labels for values in the column Sex
gender_labels = {
    'M': 'Male',
    'F': 'Female',
}

# define data set
ds = PdDataSet(
    df,
    label={
        'Name': "Name in upper case",
        'Age': "Age as binary",
        'Sex': "Gender as label",
    },
    format={
        'Name': lambda s: s.upper(),
        'Sex': lambda s: gender_labels.get(s),
        'Age':"{0:b}",
        'Height': '{:12.3f}',
        'Weight': '{:12.0f}',
    },
)
proc_print(ds, template='seaside')

The beauty of SAS format is capability to handle unknown and missing values (by presenting them by predefined string values) or map ranges of values to labels for such bands.
For that reason, pyLikeSAS provides lookup_format function capable to define custom formatting functions that cover handling of data ranges, the special label for missing value along with the label for unknown values (i.e. values not specifies in the format definition).
SAS gurus are capable to define SAS format that use function output as value label. The same functionality is easy in python - it allows to define mapping functions for values, data ranges (including low label), and of course for missing and unknown values.
Little bit elaborate example of these capabilities follows. Let start by appending some missing and unknown data to out sample dataframe:
# read data to dataframe
df0 = pd.read_csv("./data/class.csv").head(3)

# add various missing/unknown values
new_row1 = ['Old one', "", 99, np.nan, 12345.75]
dfm1 = pd.DataFrame(new_row1).T
dfm1.columns = df0.columns
new_row2 = [None, '', 14.5, np.nan, np.nan]
dfm2 = pd.DataFrame(new_row2).T
dfm2.columns = df0.columns
new_row3 = ["", 'X', np.nan, np.nan, np.nan]
dfm3 = pd.DataFrame(new_row3).T
dfm3.columns = df0.columns
df = pd.concat([dfm1, df0, dfm2, dfm3], ignore_index=True)
df['ori_sex'] = df['Sex']
df['ori_age'] = df['Age']
print(df)
which generates following dataframe:
      Name  Sex   Age Height    Weight ori_sex ori_age
0  Old one        99    NaN  12345.75              99
1   Alfred   M    14   69.0     112.5       M      14
2    Alice   F    13   56.5      84.0       F      13
3  Barbara   F    13   65.3      98.0       F      13
4     None      14.5    NaN       NaN            14.5
5            X   NaN    NaN       NaN       X     NaN
With missing and empty values in some columns user defined formatting functions should handle missing (None) values and cover all possible inputs.
Following is the example of the (rather weird) custom formatting function to be used for Name column (that handle a short name and None value).
# define function to be used for formatting Name column
def my_last_upper(s): 
    if s is None: 
        return 'Missing name'
    if len(s)<2 :
        return 'Short name repr(name):'+repr(s)
    else:
        return s[0:-1]+s[-1:].upper()

my_last_upper(None)     #-> 'Missing name'
my_last_upper('')       #-> "Short name repr(name):''"
my_last_upper('john')  #-> 'johN'
By using lookup_format we can easily extend lamba function, that was used for formatting column Sex in the first example of formats, by the following definition of formatting function:
# provide labels for values in the column Sex
gender_labels = {
    'M': 'Male',
    'F': 'Female',
}
#extend lookup_format to handle missing/unknown values 
# define function to be used for formatting
sex_labels = lookup_format(
    lookup=gender_labels,
    missing=lambda s: 'repr(sex) is:'+repr(s),
    unknown=lambda s: 'unknown value:'+str(s)
)
sex_labels('F')     # -> Female
sex_labels('M')     # -> Male
sex_labels('X')     # -> unknown value:X
sex_labels('')      # -> "repr(sex) is:''"
sex_labels(None)    # 'repr(sex) is:None'
If we want to format column Age by labels representing ranges of values, we can easily use lookup_format with lookup dictionary, that defines boundaries of required ranges, the low parameter that is used for values bellow the first range boundary (and also defines label for missing value).
# define function to be used for formatting Age column into bands
age_bands = lookup_format(
    lookup={
        13: 'Junior:13->15',
        15: 'Cadet: 15->20',
        20: 'Adult: 20->25',
        25: lambda s: 'Adult 25+:'+str(s),
    },
    low='under 14',
    missing='Age is missing',
)
age_bands(9)        # -> under 14
age_bands(14)       # -> 'Junior:13->15'
age_bands(14.5)     # -> 'Junior:13->15'
age_bands(15)       # -> 'Cadet: 15->20'
age_bands(19)       # ->'Cadet: 15->20'
age_bands(20)       # -> 'Cadet: 15->20'
age_bands(26)       # -> 'Adult 25+:26'
age_bands(None)     #-> Age is missing
All these custom formatting functions could be used in format definition of PdDataset object.
pds = PdDataSet(
    df,
    format={
        "Name": my_last_upper,
        "Sex": sex_labels,
        "Age": age_bands,
    },
    label={'Name': "Student's First Name", 'Sex': "Student Gender"},
)
PdDataset object with formats could be rendered by proc_print (the proc_print’s parameter missing defines representation of missing values that are not handled by the custom formatting functions):

proc_print(pds, keep='name sex age ori_sex ori_age Height', template='html',
           missing=('proc_print:numerical_none', 'proc_print:string_none'))

All PdDataSet constructor parameters are kept in _attrs attribute of pd.DataFrame and so these could be checked by the vars() function:
df1 = PdDataSet(
    df.head(3),
    desc='this is dataset description',
    format={
        "Name": my_upper
    },
    label={'Name': "Student's First Name", 'Sex': "Student Gender"}
)
vars(df1)

Partial output of vars statement:

{
  #...(more items)
  Items: Index(['Name', 'Sex', 'Age', 'Height', 'Weight'], dtype='object')
  #...(more items)
  '_attrs': {'desc': "this is dataset description",
  'label': {'Name': "Student's First Name", 'Sex': "Student Gender"},
  'format': {'Name': <function __main__.my_upper(s)>}},
  #...(more items)
}

After downloading, the CSV file can be read into a pd.DataFrame using the following code:
# import core of pyLikeSAS 
import pandas as pd
from pyLikeSAS.PdDataSet import PdDataSet

title: Running Data Step with PdDataSet
text:
Ultimate goal of SAS data processing (apart from SQLs nad PROCs) is running data step. Data step is basically sequential processing of (rows of) input dataset during which variables (columns) are modified/appended with complex logic frequently based on the following data step rules:

every input row is processed independently (variables are read from input table). Order in which input rows are processed is defined by by parameter - that lists ordering variables.
the new derived variables could be defined in data step - but these has to be set within processing of one row in data step. Their state is reset for the next row of input.
only variables that keep their state during whole iteration over input rows are retained variables (variables defined only within data step - not read from input dataset). Retain variables should be defined as such and their initial value is usually set at the start of processing.
There is special (automatic) variable _n_ that is order of input row that is currently processed.
Similar to the (automatic) variable _n_ are (automatic) flag variables first._order_variable_ (and last._order_variable_) that indicates that particular ordering variable has the next value in the groups in sorted input rows (or has the last value in the groups). These variables allow to define complex group by processing only partially available in SQL window function.
row is (conditionally) written to output that could consist from subset of input variables/derived/retained variables.

Data step programming is very useful concept - when fully mastered it is hard to beat its simplicity,clarity and debugging enablement (when compared to SQL or DataFrame frameworks). Its implementation in pyLikeSAS is based on definition of python function that represent data step logic. That function is set as functional parameter of pyLikeSAS.data_step() function - that allows to define input/output data sets (and their dataset options), required ordering of input data set, retained variables, etc.
There should be two values returned by data step logic function, one is True/False flag indications that processed output row should be preserved/dropped. Current implementation supports just one output data set (and one input dataset)

that could be overcome by using sql parameter in the data set options of input data set.

(this is currently weakest point of pyLikeSAS - should be changed to allow more output rows from one call of data step logic function).



Simplest example follows (note use of data set option on PdDataSet objects):
def simple_BMI(
        # here we list all input variables (read from input data set) 
        Name,  Age,  Height, Weight,
        # here we are adding retain variables (with initial values)
        ith_14plus,
        # here are all automatic variables (to be optionally in data step logic)
        _n_
):
    #    define conversion to cm/kg and set BMI for one row of class dataframe 
    #   + some game resembling SAS data step goodies
    # https://www.calculatorsoup.com/calculators/health/bmi-calculator.php


    Height = Height*2.54/100
    Weight = Weight*0.45359237
    # here we set value  new (potentially) output variables 
    # (defined in data step  logic)    
    # record input order into new variable
    nOBS = _n_+1  #SAS style nobs...
    BMI = Weight/(Height)**2
    # only cases older than 14 years are kept in output dataset
    if Age>14:
        # usage of retain variable
        ith_14plus += 1
        return True,locals()
    else:
        return 0, locals()

# read input df
df = PdDataSet(pd.read_csv("./data/class.csv").head(18))
df

dfout = data_step(
    df.ds_options(drop='sex'),
    data_step_code_function=simple_BMI,
    # keep list of variables to be passed to output dataset
    keep=[
        'nOBS', 'Name', 'Age',    'BMI','ith_14plus'
    ],
    retain={'ith_14plus':0},
    format={ 'ith_14plus': "{:.1f}"}
)

proc_print(dfout.ds_options(format={'BMI': "{:.1f}"}),template='seaside')
which of course generates output data set similar to SQL result shown in example of data set options. Its additional variable ith_14plus would be available in SQL example only after using some window functions (or aggregations).

Second - more complex - data step example demonstrates usage of following features of data step (implementation in pyLikeSAS):

by processing including first/last flag variables
usage of retain variables
using native python object in the data step logic

"""
    test of pyLikeSAS.data_step using BMI index calculation
    and other bag of SAS tricks
"""


# function that converts BMI to message 
def bmi_label(value):
    # Function to categorize weight :
    if value < 18.5:
        return "Underweight"
    elif 18.5 <= value < 25.0:
        return "Normal"
    elif 25.0 <= value < 40:
        return "Overweight"
    else:
        return "Obese"


def inch_pounds(
        # data step automatic variables
        _n_, first, last, *,
        # input data set variables + retain variables
        Name,  Age, Sex, Height, Weight, sumBMI, BMI, nOBS
):
    """
        define conversion to cm/kg and set BMI for one row of class dataframe 
        + some game resembling SAS data step goodies
         _n_, first, last, * are comnpulsory "SAS automatic" variables
        the rest of var are
            -  columns of the input dataframe (in any order)
            - retain variables  (see retain parameter of data_step function)
    """
    nOBS = _n_+1  # SAS style nobs...
    Height = Height*2.54/100
    Weight = Weight*0.45359237
    BMI = Weight/(Height)**2
    msg = bmi_label(BMI)
    # skip row with Mary
    if Name == 'Mary':
        return 0, locals()

    Surname = surname_dict.get(Name, 'n/a')
    if Surname == 'n/a':
        surname_dict[Name] = 'Unknown aged:'+str(Age)
    if Sex == 'M':
        Name = 'Mr. '+Name
    else:
        Name = 'Miss '+Name

    #  first/last processing: note nice  SAS syntax and their immutability
    # print(type(first.Sex))
    if first.Sex:
        sumBMI = 0.
    sumBMI = sumBMI+BMI
    first_Sex = first.Sex
    # print(type(first_Sex))
    first_Age = first.Age
    last_Sex = last.Sex
    last_Age = last.Age
    return 1, locals()


# read input df
df = pd.read_csv("./data/class.csv")
df

# define objects to be used pythonically in data step logic inch_pounds
surname_dict = {'Alice': 'Caroll', 'James': 'Joyce'}

dfout = data_step(
    df,
    data_step_code_function=inch_pounds,
    retain={'BMI': 0, 'sumBMI': 0, 'nOBS': 0},
    # output dataframe columns order is defined by order in function or by keep
    keep=[
        'nOBS', 'Sex', 'Age',  'Name', 'Surname',  'BMI', 'sumBMI',
        'first_Sex', 'first_Age', 'last_Sex', 'last_Age'
    ],
    label={'Name': "Student's First Name", 'Sex': "Student Gender"},
    # or use drop=['Sex'],
    by=['Sex', 'Age'],
    by_order=[True, False]
)
proc_print(dfout)
Output dataset contains BMI, cumulative sum of BMI (over gender), first/last flag variables, all sorted by sex and (descending) age.

Above pyLikeSAS data step example could be written in SAS as follows:
/* SAS code for BMI */

proc format;
      value bmi_label
      /*    # Function to categorize weight :*/
      low - 18.5 = "Underweight"
      18.5 < - 25.0 = "Normal"
      25.0 < - 40 = "Overweight"
      other = "Obese"
;
run;


proc sort data = sashelp.class out = class;
      by sex descending age;
run;

data dfout;
      length name $50;
      retain BMI 0 sumBMI 0 nOBS;

      set class (obs=19 rename=(name=name_ori));
      by sex descending age;

      drop name_ori;

      nOBS = _n_;
      Height = Height*2.54/100;
      Weight = Weight*0.45359237;
      BMI = Weight/(Height)**2;

      msg = put(BMI,bmi_label.);

      /*     skip row with Mary*/
      if name_ori='Mary' then do;
            return;
      end;

      /*    surname dictionary could be implemented using hash...*/
      /*    Surname = surname_dict.get(Name, 'n/a')*/
      /*    if Surname == 'n/a':*/
      /*        surname_dict[Name] = 'Unknown aged:'+str(Age)*/
      if Sex = 'M' then
          Name = 'Mr. ' || name_ori;
      else
          Name = 'Miss ' || name_ori;

      if first.Sex then do;
          sumBMI = 0.;
      end;
      sumBMI = sumBMI+BMI;
      first_Sex = first.Sex;
      first_Age = first.Age;
      last_Sex = last.Sex;
      last_Age = last.Age;
      output;
run;
proc print;run;
SAS code is remarkably similar - just update of surname dictionary was not implemented (could be done using SAS hash object).

Above pyLikeSAS data step example could be written in SAS as follows:
/* SAS code for BMI */

proc format;
      value bmi_label
      /*    # Function to categorize weight :*/
      low - 18.5 = "Underweight"
      18.5 < - 25.0 = "Normal"
      25.0 < - 40 = "Overweight"
      other = "Obese"
;
run;


proc sort data = sashelp.class out = class;
      by sex descending age;
run;

data dfout;
      length name $50;
      retain BMI 0 sumBMI 0 nOBS;

      set class (obs=19 rename=(name=name_ori));
      by sex descending age;

      drop name_ori;

      nOBS = _n_;
      Height = Height*2.54/100;
      Weight = Weight*0.45359237;
      BMI = Weight/(Height)**2;

      msg = put(BMI,bmi_label.);

      /*     skip row with Mary*/
      if name_ori='Mary' then do;
            return;
      end;

      /*    surname dictionary could be implemented using hash...*/
      /*    Surname = surname_dict.get(Name, 'n/a')*/
      /*    if Surname == 'n/a':*/
      /*        surname_dict[Name] = 'Unknown aged:'+str(Age)*/
      if Sex = 'M' then
          Name = 'Mr. ' || name_ori;
      else
          Name = 'Miss ' || name_ori;

      if first.Sex then do;
          sumBMI = 0.;
      end;
      sumBMI = sumBMI+BMI;
      first_Sex = first.Sex;
      first_Age = first.Age;
      last_Sex = last.Sex;
      last_Age = last.Age;
      output;
run;
proc print;run;
SAS code is remarkably similar - just update of surname dictionary was not implemented (could be done using SAS hash object).

title: Purpose
text:
After more than 20 years of using the SAS System, when I started programming in Python I felt that I had lost many of the great features and capabilities that SAS provided.
SAS workloads typically read or update datasets and then produce output datasets or reports in various formats, while logging all details to the log file.
Replicating this workflow in my early Python code was not easy. I also struggled with changing Python versions and module dependencies, switching between development in Jupyter notebooks and scheduled batch jobs in plain Python scripts, and dealing with headaches related to output formats. And of course, the SAS data step was really missing in the pandas world.
These were the main motivations for creating the pyLikeSAS package.
And yes — the current pyLikeSAS implements a Pythonic version of SAS‑like datasets, the data step, ODS, and a few simple procedures.




to have fun along the way
to implement SAS‑like formats and column labels on top of a DataFrame
to implement SAS dataset options on top of a DataFrame
to implement a function that processes a DataFrame in a SAS‑data‑step‑like manner
to implement the capability to generate various outputs (formats/styles) in a SAS ODS‑like fashion
to allow the pyLikeSAS package to be used both in Jupyter notebooks and as plain Python source files, with automatic switching of output modes
to gradually implement a few useful SAS PROCs as Python functions





performance (it could be improved — or even tested…)
support for date/time columns (in the simple, limited, but elegant SAS style)
multiple input/output datasets in a single data step
user‑defined styles in PyODS
PyODS output formatting (currently printed as Python dicts — pretty‑printed, not JSON)
graphical output support using the terminalplot package
any kind of RTF format support
many more items not yet discovered…




pyLikeSAS is my toy project. It began as part of my Python learning and development journey during the Covid‑19 years, followed by several years of only sporadic progress in adopting Python. Now that I am retired, I finally have time to tinker with a project like this (which is definitely unusual in today’s AI‑driven world).
Over time, pyLikeSAS has evolved into a learning resource aimed at two distinct groups. pyLikeSAS can help to:

improve the Python skills of hard‑core SAS users (after all, a real programmer can write Fortran code in any language in any language), and
demonstrate the strengths of SAS to Pythonic “cake‑eaters” (For programmers born after 1990, a more readable version is here).

Hopefully, pyLikeSAS enables both groups to jump into Python‑based projects while staying confident that several things that work so well in SAS will remain fully under their control — and safely isolated from the ever‑changing versions of Python and its modules.
In a way, a tiny version of my dreamed‑of involvement in the development of the original SAS System did happen — just in Python 3.12 instead of Lattice C. I tapped into the vast ecosystem of Python open‑source modules, starting from a very different place than standard C libraries. And of course, I simply followed SAS functionality rather than reinventing all that great old SAS stuff myself.
I didn’t need to worry about performance or customers — it was all about my own learning and fun.



In any case, the pyLikeSAS package is not intended for use in critical production code. That is why I am including this disclaimer (the same one I used in the old days, even on paid customer projects):
This software is not designed or intended for use in the on‑line control of aircraft, air traffic, aircraft navigation, or aircraft communications, or in the design, construction, operation, or maintenance of any nuclear facility.

Data set options in SAS is great facility that is capable to reduce row/columns of dataset on the fly when it is used as input for processing (or at the end of processing for output dataset).
PdDataSet method ds_options supports similar functionality - it returns PdDataSet object that is modified by specified dataset options.
In addition to traditional SAS dataset options it is possible to work directly with the results of SQL select statement that is processes on parent PdDataSet object (or even use more PdDataSet objects). It resembles inline SQL input available in SAS DS2 procedure.

Dataset option sql is not limited to parent table self. It is possible to add several more “tables” into SQL statement using ducktbls list (of PdDataSet/Pd.DataFrame objects) which are identifies by their aliases defined by ducktbls_aliases list (of strings).
Following elaborate example links 3 tables (additional tables are not necessary the same as self as it is in this demonstration case):
# using sql with more tables
dfdset=PdDataSet(df)
df1 = dfdset
df2 = dfdset
dfdset.ds_options(
  sql="""
        SELECT
          a.*,df1.*,xdf2.*
        FROM
          self AS a,
          df1,
          xdf2
        WHERE
            a.name <> df1.name
            AND a.name <> xdf2.name
          AND a.age < df1.age
          AND df1.age < xdf2.age
          AND a.height < df1.height
          AND df1.height < xdf2.height
          AND a.weight > df1.weight
          AND df1.weight > xdf2.weight
      """,
  ducktbls=[df1, df2],
  ducktbls_aliases=['df1', 'xdf2']
)

title: PdDataSet
text:
PdDataSet





Name
Description




PdDataSet
PdDataSet Class - implementation of SASlike data set including dataset options on top of pandas DataFrame






PdDataSet.PdDataSet(data, *args, desc=None, label=None, format=None, **kwargs)
PdDataSet Class - implementation of SASlike data set including dataset options on top of pandas DataFrame





Name
Description




ds_options
method that implements SAS like dataset options for PdDataSet object.





PdDataSet.PdDataSet.ds_options(
    obs=None,
    firstobs=None,
    where=None,
    sql=None,
    keep=None,
    drop=None,
    rename=None,
    label=None,
    format=None,
    desc=None,
    sqlengine='duck',
    ducktbls=None,
    ducktbls_aliases=None,
)
method that implements SAS like dataset options for PdDataSet object.
there is an example::











Name
Type
Description
Default




obs
int | tuple | list[tuple] | None
Defines selection of observations (rows). Examples of obs parameter usage: - Pass tuple with from-to values for example:obs=(0,4) - or list of from-to tuples, for example:obs=[(1,5),(12,-1),...] - Use (n,None) for reading from n-th row to the last row.
None


firstobs
int | None
Sets first observation to read, obs then should be int and represents last observation.
None


where
str | None
SQL where clause to be applied data set.
None


sql
str | None
SQL select statement be applied. Use self as table name for the parent DataFrame or use other DataFrames as names of the other tables.
None


keep
str | list[str] | None
list of columns to keep.
None


drop
str | list[str] | None
list of columns to drop.
None


rename
dict[str, str] | None
Dictionary that defines renaming of column names Example:: rename= { ‘oriname1’:‘newname1’, ‘oriname2’:‘newname2’,… ‘orinameN’:‘newnameN’ }
None


label
dict[str, str] | None
Dictionary that sets labelsof columns to be used when printed Example:: label= { ‘colname1’:‘long label for colname1’, ‘colname2’:‘longest label for colname2’,… }
None


format
dict[str, str | Callable] | None
Dictionary thatsets formats of columns to be used when printed Example:: format= { ‘colname1’:formatting_function, ‘colname2’:‘{:,.0f}’,… } formatting_function is a callable function that takes a value and returns a formatted string.
None


desc
str | None
sets descriptive label of dataset.
None


sqlengine
str
name of SQL engine duckdb or pandasql (pandasql is used only if not set to default duckdb)
'duck'


ducktbls
list[pd.DataFrame] | None
List of DataFrames to be passed to duckdb
None


ducktbls_aliases
list[str] | None
List of DataFrame aliases to be used in sql parameter as duckdb table names.
None














Name
Type
Description





Self
pandas DataFrame enriched by labels + formats + descritption







Usage of obs for selection of observations::

Pass tuple with from-to values for example:obs=(0,4)
or list of from-to tuples, for example:obs=[(1,5),(12,-1),...]
Use (n,None) for reading from n-th row to the last row.


#| echo: true
#| eval: false
    ds=pd.Dataframe(..)
    myDset=ds.ds_options(
        obs=(0,4),
        where="age>14",
        sql="select * from self where sex='F'",
        keep=['Name', 'Age']
        )
